section.title: Preparations for the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Standing on the very threshing floor where judgment had just been stayed, David names the site as the future house of the LORD and throws himself into massive preparation, gathering stone, iron, bronze, and cedar in staggering quantities. He then charges Solomon directly, explaining why he himself, a man of war and bloodshed, cannot build it, and why his son, a man of rest whose very name means peace, is the one appointed to do so. Finally he charges all Israel's leaders to help. For the exiles rebuilding their own much smaller temple, this chapter is a model of wholehearted, costly devotion to God's house.
section.thesis: Barred from building the temple himself because he was a man of war and bloodshed, David pours out everything he has, wealth, materials, and a father's charge, to ensure his son Solomon, the man of rest whose name means peace, can build it, teaching every generation that follows, exiles included, to give themselves as fully to God's house as David gave, even when the privilege of building falls to someone else.
section.frameNote: Six beats: David designates the site, verse 1; gathering materials on a massive scale, verses 2 to 5; David's charge to Solomon explaining why not him, verses 6 to 10; the charge to be strong and courageous, verses 11 to 13; the resources provided, verses 14 to 16; and the charge to the leaders of Israel, verses 17 to 19.

units.label: David designates the site
units.note: here shall be the house of the LORD

verses.text: Then David said, "Here shall be the {{bayit|house}} of the LORD God, as well as the altar of burnt offering for Israel."

units.label: Gathering materials on a massive scale
units.note: iron, bronze, and cedar beyond counting

verses.text: So David gave orders to gather the foreigners in the land of Israel, from whom he appointed stonecutters to prepare finished stones for building the house of God.
David provided a large quantity of iron to make the nails for the doors of the gateways and for the fittings, together with more bronze than could be weighed
and more cedar logs than could be counted; for the Sidonians and Tyrians had brought a large quantity of cedar logs to David.

And David said, "My son Solomon is young and inexperienced, and the house to be built for the LORD must be exceedingly magnificent, famous and glorious throughout all lands. Therefore I must make preparations for it." So David made lavish preparations before his death.

units.label: David's charge to Solomon: why not you

verses.text: Then David called for his son Solomon and instructed him to build a house for the LORD, the God of Israel.
"My son," said David to Solomon, "it was in my heart to build a house for the Name of the LORD my God,
but this word of the LORD came to me: 'You have shed much blood and waged great wars. You are not to build a house for My Name because you have shed so much blood on the ground before Me.

But a son will be born to you who will be a man of {{menuchah|rest}}. I will give him rest from all his enemies on every side; for his name will be Solomon, and I will grant to Israel {{shalom|peace}} and quiet during his reign.
He is the one who will build a house for My Name. He will be My son, and I will be his Father. And I will establish the throne of his kingdom over Israel forever.'

units.label: Be strong and courageous
units.note: may the LORD give you insight

verses.text: Now, my son, may the LORD be with you, and may you succeed in building the house of the LORD your God, as He said you would.
Above all, may the LORD give you insight and understanding when He puts you in command over Israel, so that you may keep the Law of the LORD your God.
Then you will succeed, if you carefully follow the statutes and ordinances that the LORD commanded Moses for Israel. Be {{chazaq|strong}} and courageous. Do not be afraid or discouraged.

Now behold, I have taken great pains to provide for the house of the LORD, 100,000 talents of gold, 1,000,000 talents of silver, and bronze and iron too great to be weighed. I have also provided timber and stone, and you may add to them.
You also have many workers: stonecutters, masons, carpenters, and men skilled in every kind of work,
in gold and silver, bronze and iron, craftsmen beyond number. Now begin the work, and may the LORD be with you.

units.label: The charge to the leaders of Israel
units.note: set your heart and soul to seek the LORD

verses.text: Then David ordered all the leaders of Israel to help his son Solomon:

"Is not the LORD your God with you, and has He not granted you rest on every side? For He has given the inhabitants of the land into my hand, and the land has been subdued before the LORD and His people.

Now set your heart and soul to seek the LORD your God. Begin building the sanctuary of the LORD God, so that you may bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD and the holy articles of God into the temple that will be built for the Name of the LORD."

bayit.gloss: House, in the sense of a building for God's presence, echoing the same word played on in the Davidic covenant, 1 Chronicles 17, where God promised to build David a house, a dynasty. Here David designates the physical bayit that will house the ark and answer that covenant promise in stone and cedar.

menuchah.gloss: Rest, quietness, settledness. God promises that Solomon will be a man of menuchah, given rest from all his enemies, in deliberate contrast to David, a man of war whose reign was marked by constant campaigning; a temple of God's dwelling fittingly belongs to an era of rest, not ongoing battle.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being. The name Solomon, Shelomoh, is built on this very root, and God promises Israel will have peace and quiet during his reign, the fitting condition under which the house of peace, the temple, can finally be built.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, courageous. David's charge to Solomon, 'be strong and courageous, do not be afraid or discouraged,' echoes almost word for word the LORD's charge to Joshua at the start of the conquest, Joshua 1:6-9, framing the temple's construction as a task requiring the same covenant courage as entering the promised land.

basic.context: Standing on the threshing floor where the plague was just stopped, David immediately names the site as the future house of the LORD and throws himself into massive preparation, gathering stonecutters, more iron and bronze than could be weighed, and more cedar than could be counted. He explains to Solomon directly why he himself cannot build the temple: God told him that because he had shed much blood in war, he was not the one to build a house for God's Name. Instead, a son would be born who would be a man of rest, Solomon, whose very name means peace, and who would build the house and receive the everlasting covenant promise. David charges Solomon to be strong and courageous and to keep God's law carefully, then hands over a staggering amount of gold, silver, and materials, telling Solomon he may add to them and simply begin the work. Finally David charges all the leaders of Israel to help Solomon, urging them to set their heart and soul to seek the LORD. Key questions: why couldn't David, God's beloved king, build the temple? What does it mean that Solomon is 'a man of rest'? And what does David's example teach about giving generously toward a task someone else will finish?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording David's specific instructions and the reasons given for why Solomon, not David, would build the temple. It describes what happened and what was said, not a general prescription that all builders of war are permanently disqualified from all sacred work; the point is specific to this particular house and this particular moment in redemptive history. What endures for every reader is the pattern of wholehearted, generous devotion to God's house, and the picture of a father preparing everything he can for a task he will not live to see completed.
basic.originalLanguages: David designates the bayit, the house, that answers the covenant promise of chapter 17. God explains that Solomon will be a man of menuchah, rest, and that his reign will bring shalom, peace, a wordplay built into Solomon's very name, Shelomoh. David charges his son to be chazaq, strong and courageous, using almost the same words God once gave Joshua before entering the promised land.
basic.typology: David, the man of war who cannot build the temple, and Solomon, the man of rest who does, together anticipate the two comings of Christ: the conquering King who wars against sin and every enemy, and the Prince of Peace who builds the true and lasting house of God, His church, in an era of settled peace secured by His victory.
basic.christ: The everlasting throne promised again here, 'I will establish the throne of his kingdom over Israel forever,' echoes and extends the covenant of chapter 17, ultimately fulfilled in Christ. Jesus is both the man of war who has decisively defeated sin and death, and the man of rest who gives His people true peace and builds His true and final temple.
basic.god: God is the one who assigns tasks according to His own wisdom, granting David the privilege of preparing everything while reserving the privilege of building for Solomon. God rewards wholehearted devotion, and He is faithful to His covenant word across generations, from the promise given to David to its fulfillment through his son.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 7 records the original covenant promise this chapter builds on. Hebrews 1:5 applies the father son language of verse 10 to Christ. Matthew 12:42 says one greater than Solomon is here. Ephesians 2:19-22 describes the church as God's true, living temple.
basic.oneStory: David's preparation for a temple he will never build models wholehearted devotion to a purpose bigger than any one lifetime, while the handoff from the man of war to the man of rest anticipates the pattern completed in Christ, who conquers every enemy and then builds His true and lasting house among His people.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a series of concentric charges: David's own preparation, verses 1 to 5; his personal charge to Solomon, verses 6 to 16; and his charge to the nation's leaders, verses 17 to 19, each widening the circle of responsibility for the temple project. The explanatory word to Solomon, verses 8 to 10, functions as the theological center, explaining both David's exclusion and Solomon's appointment.
context.historical: The vast quantities named, 100,000 talents of gold and 1,000,000 talents of silver, roughly 3,770 tons of gold and 37,700 tons of silver by the footnote's estimate, are staggering even allowing for ancient hyperbolic or symbolic use of round numbers, underscoring the scale of David's devotion rather than functioning as a precise modern audit. Foreign craftsmen, Sidonian and Tyrian cedar suppliers among them, reflect the same kind of international cooperation later seen fully in Solomon's actual building project, 1 Kings 5.
context.canonical: This chapter directly answers the promise of 2 Samuel 7 and 1 Chronicles 17, that David's offspring would build the house and receive an everlasting throne. Solomon's charge to 'be strong and courageous' deliberately echoes Joshua 1:6-9, framing temple building as a task of the same spiritual magnitude as the original conquest of the land.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain, directly from David's own mouth, why he could not build the temple and why Solomon would, to record the scale of David's preparation as a model of wholehearted generosity, and to charge both Solomon and the nation's leaders to seek the LORD fully in completing the work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical reason, that David's wars disqualified him from this particular building task, not a universal principle that soldiers or those who have used force are permanently barred from serving God. What is prescriptive and abiding is the call to wholehearted devotion, careful obedience to God's word, and courageous, unafraid service, values David explicitly charges onto Solomon and the leaders.

debates.question: Why could David not build the temple, given that his wars were, according to 1 Chronicles 18, the LORD's own doing?

views.label: A matter of fittingness, not personal guilt
views.case: The text never calls David's warfare sinful; earlier chapters explicitly credit the LORD with giving David victory, 18:6, 13. The reasoning given here is about symbolic fittingness: a house dedicated to God's Name and to peaceful worship suits an era and a builder marked by rest, not one whose reign, however righteous, was necessarily marked by bloodshed and war.

views.label: A real, if not condemning, disqualification tied to bloodshed
views.case: Others note that Numbers 35:33 and similar texts treat bloodshed, even justified bloodshed, as something that can defile land and require atonement; on this reading, David's extensive bloodshed genuinely disqualifies him from this holy task, not as personal sin needing forgiveness, but as a real barrier analogous to ceremonial restrictions elsewhere in the Law.

debates.bearing: Both views agree this is not a rebuke of David's character; God still calls him 'My servant' and lavishly credits his devotion. The distinction instead pictures two necessary stages, the man of war who establishes the kingdom and the man of rest who builds its house of worship, a pattern completed in Christ, who both conquers as warrior King and builds as Prince of Peace.

debates.question: Is the promise given to Solomon here, 'I will be his Father, and he will be My son, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever,' primarily about Solomon or does it deliberately point beyond him?

views.label: Primarily historical, describing Solomon's own reign
views.case: In its immediate context this promise explains why Solomon specifically was chosen and equipped to build the temple and reign in peace; the language of sonship reflects ancient Near Eastern royal adoption language applied to the reigning king, fulfilled literally in Solomon's forty year reign of unprecedented peace and building.

views.label: Intentionally messianic, reaching beyond Solomon
views.case: The promise of an eternal throne cannot be limited to Solomon, whose kingdom later divided and fell; this language directly extends the everlasting covenant of 1 Chronicles 17:11-14 and is applied by Hebrews 1:5 directly to Christ, showing that the Chronicler and later Scripture both read this promise as ultimately messianic.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: the promise is genuinely and immediately about Solomon, who did build the temple and reign in peace, while its everlasting scope necessarily reaches beyond him to Christ, the true and final Son of David whose throne truly has no end.

study.originalLanguages: David names the bayit, the house, on the very ground where mercy had just met judgment. God explains that Solomon, unlike David the man of battle, will be a man of menuchah, rest, and his reign will be marked by shalom, peace, a wordplay woven into his very name, Shelomoh, meaning peaceable. David's charge to Solomon, 'be chazaq, strong, and courageous,' echoes almost verbatim the charge God gave Joshua at the outset of the conquest, tying the building of the temple to the same order of covenant faithfulness as entering the promised land.

typology.type: David, the man of war, excluded from building God's house
typology.fulfillment: Christ's first coming as the warrior King who conquers sin, death, and every hostile power, Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: Solomon, the man of rest whose name means peace, chosen to build
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Prince of Peace, who builds His true and lasting temple, the church, in the peace secured by His finished victory, Isaiah 9:6, Ephesians 2:19-22.

typology.type: The everlasting throne promised again to Solomon
typology.fulfillment: The throne of Christ, the Son of David, whose kingdom truly has no end, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:5.

study.christ: David, the man of war whose reign of conquest secured Israel's borders, and Solomon, the man of rest whose very name means peace and who was appointed to build God's house, together form a pattern completed in Jesus Christ. He is, first, the conquering King who has already won the decisive victory over sin, death, and every hostile power, Colossians 2:15, and He is, second, the true Prince of Peace, Isaiah 9:6, who in the peace secured by that victory builds His own true and lasting temple, His church, indwelt by God's own Spirit, Ephesians 2:19-22. The everlasting throne promised again to Solomon here, echoing 1 Chronicles 17 and quoted of Christ in Hebrews 1:5, finds its only truly everlasting fulfillment in Him, one greater than Solomon, Matthew 12:42.
study.god: God is the one who assigns callings according to His own perfect wisdom, granting David the joy of lavish preparation while reserving for Solomon the joy of building. God's faithfulness spans generations, the promise given in 2 Samuel 7 and 1 Chronicles 17 now moving toward fulfillment through David's careful obedience and Solomon's coming labor. And God delights in wholehearted devotion, whether shown in David's staggering generosity or in the charge for Israel's leaders to seek Him with heart and soul.

crossReferences.note: The original covenant promise that David's offspring would build the house and receive an everlasting throne, which this chapter directly builds on and restates.
God's charge to Joshua to be strong and courageous, echoed almost word for word in David's charge to Solomon, framing the temple project with conquest-level covenant seriousness.
Applies the father-son language of verse 10 directly to Christ, the true and final Son of David.
The church as God's true and living temple, built together in Christ, the ultimate fulfillment of the house David and Solomon prepared and built.

study.oneStory: Barred from building the temple because he was a man of war, David nonetheless pours out everything he has, gold, silver, materials, and a father's careful charge, to ensure his son Solomon, the man of rest whose name means peace, can build it. That handoff from the man of war to the man of rest anticipates the fuller pattern completed in Christ, the conquering King who is also the Prince of Peace, who builds His people into God's true and everlasting house.

quiz.q: Why did God tell David he could not build the temple, according to David's own words to Solomon?

options.text: Because David had shed much blood and waged great wars, so he was not to build a house for God's Name.
options.note: Verse 8 records David's direct quotation of the reason God gave him.

options.text: Because David had committed a specific unforgivable sin that disqualified him from all sacred service.
options.note: The text never presents this as an unforgivable sin; God still calls David His servant and grants him the joy of massive preparation for the temple.

options.text: Because Solomon had already been publicly anointed king before David could begin.
options.note: The stated reason concerns David's warfare and bloodshed, not the timing of Solomon's anointing.

options.text: Because David lacked the necessary skilled workers and materials to begin.
options.note: The opposite is true; David gathered vast materials and workers, and this was not the stated reason he could not build, which was specifically his history of war and bloodshed.

quiz.q: What does God promise about Solomon that explains why he, rather than David, would build the temple?

options.text: That Solomon would be a man of rest, given peace and quiet during his reign, fitting to build a house of peaceful worship.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly contrasts Solomon, a man of rest, with David's reign of war.

options.text: That Solomon would inherit David's military skill and continue his wars.
options.note: The opposite: Solomon is specifically promised rest from war, not a continuation of David's military campaigns.

options.text: That Solomon would be wiser than any king who had lived before him.
options.note: Solomon's wisdom is a major theme elsewhere, but this specific passage's stated reason is his being a man of rest and peace, not primarily his wisdom.

options.text: That Solomon would reign only briefly before the kingdom divided.
options.note: This anticipates a later historical development but is not the reason given here for why Solomon, rather than David, would build the temple.

quiz.q: How much did David personally provide toward the temple's construction?

options.text: A staggering amount, 100,000 talents of gold and 1,000,000 talents of silver, along with iron, bronze, timber, and stone beyond counting.
options.note: Verse 14 records these vast figures, showing the scale of David's lavish devotion.

options.text: A modest sum, since most of the resources came from Solomon's own later efforts.
options.note: The chapter presents David's contribution as staggeringly large, not modest, with Solomon invited simply to add to it, verse 14.

options.text: Only the site itself, purchased from Ornan, with no further materials provided.
options.note: David provided far more than the site alone; verses 2-5 and 14-16 detail massive additional materials and skilled workers.

options.text: A large sum of gold and silver, though the exact quantities are left unspecified in the text.
options.note: The text is not vague here; it gives specific, staggering figures for gold and silver in verse 14.

quiz.q: What charge did David give Solomon regarding obedience to God's law?

options.text: To carefully follow the statutes and ordinances the LORD commanded Moses, and to be strong and courageous, not afraid or discouraged.
options.note: Verses 12-13 give this charge, echoing the language given to Joshua before the conquest, Joshua 1:6-9.

options.text: To rely only on his own wisdom and insight without reference to God's law.
options.note: David specifically charges Solomon to keep the Law of the LORD, not to rely on wisdom alone apart from it.

options.text: To seek the LORD's blessing but not to worry about following specific statutes.
options.note: David is specific, not vague, urging careful obedience to particular statutes and ordinances, not a general blessing detached from the Law.

options.text: To be strong and courageous, trusting that success would follow regardless of obedience.
options.note: David does charge Solomon to be strong and courageous, but he explicitly ties success to careful obedience to God's statutes, not courage alone.

quiz.q: What did David charge the leaders of Israel to do?

options.text: To set their heart and soul to seek the LORD and help Solomon build the sanctuary and bring in the ark and the holy articles.
options.note: Verses 17-19 record this direct charge to Israel's leaders.

options.text: To leave Solomon entirely on his own to complete the temple without assistance.
options.note: The opposite: David specifically calls the leaders to help Solomon, not to leave him unsupported.

options.text: To focus on further military conquests rather than temple construction.
options.note: David's charge is specifically about the temple project and seeking the LORD, not further conquest.

options.text: To provide financial resources only, without personal spiritual devotion required.
options.note: David's charge goes beyond resources to a call for wholehearted spiritual devotion, 'set your heart and soul to seek the LORD.'

questions: David poured out everything he had for a temple he would never see built. What are you investing in today that you may not live to see completed, and how does that shape your motivation?

David, the man of war, gives way to Solomon, the man of rest and peace. Where in your own life do you need to recognize that a season of striving must give way to a season of receiving what God provides?
David charged Solomon to be strong and courageous and to keep God's law carefully. Where do you need that same combination of courage and careful obedience right now?
David called Israel's leaders to 'set your heart and soul to seek the LORD.' What would it look like for your heart and soul, not just your resources, to be fully set on serving God this week?
Solomon's name means peace, and Christ is the true Prince of Peace who builds God's lasting house. How does resting in Christ's finished work change the way you approach your own work and service?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote explains that Solomon, Shelomoh, sounds like and is likely derived from the Hebrew word for peace, shalom, a wordplay built directly into the promise that his reign would bring Israel peace and quiet.

The BSB footnotes convert the ancient weights: 100,000 talents of gold is approximately 3,770 tons, and 1,000,000 talents of silver is approximately 37,700 tons, figures so vast that many read them as reflecting the scale of David's devotion in a culturally elevated, rounded manner rather than a precise modern audit.

David's charge to Solomon, 'be strong and courageous, do not be afraid or discouraged,' closely echoes the LORD's own charge to Joshua in Joshua 1:6-9, deliberately framing the temple project with the same covenant seriousness as the conquest of the land.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 22:1-19. Two em dashes in the source, at verses 5 and 14, and one at the join of verses 15-16, were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file. A search of Alistair Begg, Tim Keller, John Piper, Sinclair Ferguson, and D.A. Carson's available sermon archives found no message preached specifically on this chapter, so sermon status is recorded as none.
